data.title: UnitedHealth Group: Soaring On Big Profit Beat
data.description:
Article Thesis

UnitedHealth Group Incorporated (UNH) reported its most recent earnings results on Tuesday morning. The company delivered a double beat, showing much stronger than expected profitability, and raised its guidance for 2026—the market liked that, sending UNH 8% higher on the back of the good news.

Past Coverage

I have written about UnitedHealth Group here on Seeking Alpha in the past, most recently on January 27, around three months ago. I was bullish back then, noting UNH's potential for a turnaround and a profit recovery. So far, this thesis has played out well, with UNH reporting appealing profits today and with UNH trading around 23% higher than when I published my January article—a very nice return for a three-month period, I believe.

What Happened?

On Tuesday morning, UnitedHealth reported its Q1 earnings results. The headline numbers looked like this:

UNH beat the consensus estimate on both lines, which is naturally a great result. This was also a major improvement versus the previous quarter, when UNH had delivered an earnings hit and a revenue miss. The revenue growth rate wasn't especially exciting, at just 2%, but this was still better than the roughly flat revenues that analysts had predicted. On the earnings front, UNH did much better, with its Q1 earnings per share of $7.23 being the best result in years. The market reacted very positively to these results and UNH's increased guidance, sending shares higher by 8% at the time of writing—I think this is due to many investors having increased confidence that the turnaround is working.

UNH's Performance In Q1

The problems that plagued UnitedHealth in the recent past are well known—bad cost trends, relatively weak margins across its different business units, and so on resulted in major profitability headwinds compared to the peak levels we saw in 2023 to 2024. These headwinds haven't fully passed yet, but during the first quarter, there were some positive developments. Let's dive into UNH's numbers:

Looking at UnitedHealthcare, UNH's biggest unit in terms of both revenue and profit generation, we see that both of these metrics improved versus one year earlier. Revenues were up slightly, rising from $84.6 billion one year ago to a little more than $86 billion during the most recent quarter, while operating income was up by $500 million, or around 10%, over the same time frame. Operating earnings thus grew faster than revenues, which was the result of higher margins—a very positive and, I believe, highly important development. After all, UNH's key issue over the last year or so wasn't a revenue problem but a margin problem -- costs were too high and growing faster than expected, which made UNH's margins shrink despite some revenue growth. This has been reverted for now, with costs growing less than revenue in Q1, allowing for some margin expansion.

UnitedHealthcare also did reasonably well when it comes to adding customers in the Employer and Individual segment, with a little more than 400,000 people added over the last year. In the Medicare and Retirement segment, the number of insured people declined by more than 900,000—this was likely the result of higher pricing primarily. While some may interpret a lower number of insured Medicare patients as a negative, this likely helped drive margins for UnitedHealthcare during the quarter—a lower number of, on average, higher-cost, lower-margin patients (management mentions complex populations) is good for the overall margin performance.

UnitedHealth's other business unit, Optum, did not perform as well as UnitedHealthcare: revenues for this unit were down marginally compared to one year earlier, but costs were up by a couple of hundred million dollars, which caused Optum to show a $600 million smaller operating profit versus one year earlier. Optum's operating margin declined from around 6% to around 5%—an unfortunate development, but luckily, UnitedHealthcare, the unit with the much better performance, is a lot bigger, so Optum's weaker performance didn't matter too much. Looking at the different units that make up Optum, Optum Health, Optum RX, and Optum Insight, we see that Optum Health, the medical care provider business, was the worst performer, showing a revenue decline of a couple of percentage points. Optum RX and Optum Health, which are smaller relative to Optum Health, did better in Q1—both showed a revenue increase in the low single digits.

On a company-wide basis, between the nice improvements seen in UnitedHealthcare and some profit headwinds in Optum, mainly due to Optum Health, profits were relatively flat. UnitedHealth bought back some shares, however, which reduced its share count, which is why earnings per share were up versus last year's first quarter—the first year-over-year improvement seen in the recent past and a major reversal from the big earnings per share declines seen over the last couple of quarters.

Looking at the cash flow picture, we see strong cash generation in Q1: UnitedHealth's operating cash flow was $8.9 billion, with just $800 million of capital expenditures during the quarter—free cash flows thus were roughly $8 billion, or around 3% of UNH's market capitalization. Not all quarters are equally strong throughout the year, but this still sets UNH up for a good year, cash-flow-wise, I believe. UNH paid out some money via dividends but used the majority of these free cash flows to strengthen its balance sheet, improving its net debt position by several billion dollars in a single quarter.

UNH: What's The Outlook?

UnitedHealth's performance in Q1 was better than expected, and the same holds true for its fiscal 2026 guidance: Management now forecasts earnings per share of at least $18.25, significantly more than the $17.87 consensus. There is, of course, no guarantee that management's guidance is accurate, but assuming the company will indeed generate profits of $18.25 or more, 2026 would be a much stronger year than 2025, when UnitedHealth earned $16.31 on a per-share basis. If earnings per share come in ahead of $18.25, we do not yet know how much higher, so it's hard to forecast what UNH's earnings per share growth will look like this year exactly. But it seems reasonable to me to assume that earnings per share growth will easily be in the double digits, with my guess being that we will see earnings per share growth in the 15% range (the $18.25 bottom of the guidance range would be a 12% increase versus 2025). 2026 will still be a significantly weaker year compared to 2024, when UnitedHealth earned more than $27, but the trend is positive, with UNH having passed the nadir and getting its profitability back up.

UNH's shares are up substantially from the lows at around $230 to $240, but they aren't especially expensive despite the compelling recent performance—if UNH were to earn $18.50 this year, its earnings multiple would be 19. UNH isn't trading at a bargain valuation today, but if UNH continues to turn around by improving pricing and better managing costs, we should see considerable earnings growth in the coming years, too, and UNH would still have ample long-term potential, I think. Add a dividend yield of around 2.5% and some buybacks, and the longer-term setup is positive—if you believe in management's ability to manage the current turnaround successfully. I believe that UNH's Q1 earnings were definitely reassuring in that regard.

data.title: Robbing Peter To Pay Paul: Another Look At Long/Short Direct Index Tax-Loss Harvesting
data.description:
Introduction

It’s tax season, and we’ve been reading a lot about taxes – and strategies for mitigating them.1 In this note, we’ll take a close look at one such strategy, known as leveraged long/short direct index tax-loss harvesting (LSDI), and explain how investors being pitched the strategy can assess whether it’s right for them.

According to research by BlackRock, over $1 trillion in U.S. household wealth is held in single-stock positions with a low cost basis, where the owner would like to diversify but is reluctant to sell due to the immediate capital gains tax hit.2 LSDI is designed to solve this problem: it allows an investor to sell a concentrated, highly appreciated stock holding and replace it with a diversified portfolio of hundreds of stocks, without paying capital gains tax along the way. We wrote (somewhat skeptically) about this a year ago in “Out of the Frying Pan and Into the Fire,” but we’ve been hearing so much about the merits of this strategy – in the press and in conversations with our clients – that we thought we should take a closer look to see if our earlier analysis missed something.

How LSDI works

Consider an investor who was an early employee of Shopify Inc. (ticker: SHOP), and now owns $10 million of SHOP with a basis close to 0.3 This single holding represents over 80% of her investment portfolio, and she’d love to sell it and buy a nicely diversified basket of stocks instead – but she lives in the Bay Area and would face a combined Federal and California capital gains tax rate of 37%, so she’s looking for something better.

A wealth manager proposes she enter an LSDI program. The manager will place her $10 million of Shopify stock into a separately managed account (SMA), simultaneously going long $10 million of individual stocks and short $10 million of others. Over time, the manager will realize capital losses from both the long and short portfolios. Each time a position is sold, it’s replaced with another stock to keep the long and short portfolios at their target sizes, while complying with the IRS wash sale rule.4

Individual stock selection will also be influenced by tracking error constraints and a stock-picking model. It’s important to realize an important distinction between long-only tax loss harvesting and LSDI; the former need not be accompanied by stock picking, while the long/short version does require individual stock selection to get the desired tax treatment.

As realized losses accumulate, the manager sells some of the investor’s Shopify holding and replaces it with a diversified basket of stocks (the “replacement portfolio”), using the harvested losses to offset the capital gains on each Shopify sale. The manager also tax-loss harvests the diversified replacement portfolio itself.

After a while, all of the Shopify stock has been sold and the investor holds the replacement portfolio, plus an equal amount of long/short portfolio. At this point, the manager can use further harvested losses to unwind the leveraged long/short portfolio, but it will not be possible to unwind it entirely.

The investor can either continue the program indefinitely, or at some horizon, ask the manager to close out all the remaining leveraged long and short positions, generating some combination of long-term capital gains on the longs and short-term capital gains on the shorts (current tax rules require that realized gains from short positions always be treated as short-term capital gains regardless of holding period). This finally leaves the investor with what she wanted: conversion of her concentrated holding into a diversified portfolio of individual stocks with no capital gains tax paid until the long/short portfolio is unwound at her chosen horizon.

Looking under the hood

It sounds pretty good, doesn’t it? It’s also pretty complicated. It took us a while to wrap our heads around all the nuances involved, and to build a simulation model to analyze its merits (which we describe in detail in the Appendix). Before we go further, we should note that we are not tax experts and we have not had direct conversations with any of the many purveyors of this strategy, though we have reviewed with friends and clients the general contours of what is typically proposed. We should also note: if you believe the manager can pick stocks that will outperform the ones they short – while doing the tax-loss harvesting and staying on the right side of the wash sale rule – our analysis will help you decide how much outperformance you’d need to make the strategy worthwhile.

Our results depend on a few assumptions, and we want to say upfront that results depend heavily on an individual’s circumstances. For some situations, LSDI can make sense. In this note, we’re primarily going to discuss a central case with what we think are a set of typical assumptions, and most of the assumptions on markets and implementation are chosen to put the LSDI program in a favorable light. In the Appendix, we explain our simulation model in detail, and you can find the simulation results in this Google sheet.

To start, we’ll keep tax rates and rules constant over the investment horizon (37% and 54% tax rate on long-term and short-term capital gains respectively). For the stock market, we use an average single-stock volatility of 30% pa, roughly what it has been over the past five years, and average annual stock price growth of 6%.5 For the concentrated stock holding, we assume the same expected average annual return of 6%, but with higher volatility of 50%.6 We’ll assume all stocks pay no dividends. We assume the manager charges fees of 0.5% pa on the diversified long portfolio of stocks (but not on the Shopify position), and that the custodian of the SMA charges 0.75% on the long/short portfolio.7

Let’s start with a 20-year horizon – long enough to complete the transition out of the appreciated asset and to harvest enough losses to meaningfully reduce the size of the long/short portfolio. We assume that at the end of 20 years, the remaining long/short portfolio is unwound, taxes are paid, and the investor is left with a diversified holding of individual stocks, and stops paying fees to the manager.

We calculate that the investor will have earned an average after-tax, net-of-fees compound return of 2.05% pa on her initial $10 million.

What if she had simply sold her $10 million Shopify holding, paid $3.7 million in capital gains tax, and invested the remaining $6.3 million in a low-cost, broad stock market index fund? In that case, we calculate an average after-tax return of 2.16% pa on the same $10 million starting value.

The main reason the LSDI program’s expected return is 0.11% pa lower is that the fees eat up most of the tax benefit, though paying short-term capital gains tax to cover the shorts, and the volatility drag of holding SHOP for longer also hurt the LSDI program’s after-tax compound return. Over 20 years, the investor will have paid $2.7 million in fees.8 Compare this to the $3.7 million of capital gains tax she was trying to avoid, or at least defer. She’s paid less to Uncle Sam, but more than half as much to her LSDI manager. And at the end, she’ll still have to pay long-term and short-term capital gains tax to unwind the remaining long/short portfolio, plus she’ll have taken more risk over the horizon – and on top of all that, she’ll have $26 million in unrealized gains in her replacement portfolio. This is far more embedded capital gains liability than in the index fund she could have bought fresh 20 years earlier, which would have only $14 million of unrealized gains. This means that, if she needs to sell some of her assets for spending or wants to invest it in other assets, she’ll have to foot about double the tax bill as a result of going down the LSDI road.

Across 2,000 simulations, the difference in returns between the two strategies had a standard deviation of 2.9 percentage points. This means that, even if you think the manager can generate an extra 0.5% of stock-picking alpha (the average LSDI excess return will be about 0.39% pa) there would be only a 55% probability, hardly better than a coin-flip, that the LSDI program would give you a better compound return than selling your concentrated holding upfront, paying the taxes and investing in your desired portfolio.

Taking off the rose-colored glasses

To arrive at the results we described above, virtually every assumption we made was generous to an LSDI program, setting aside whether the manager can generate alpha by successfully picking stocks. Here we’ll try to get a bit more realistic:

  • Our example uses the highest state tax rate in the country. Most investors face lower combined rates than California residents, and will therefore find LSDI less attractive than our example suggests. If our investor had been a Texas resident, the LSDI program would have an annual after-tax return 0.88% worse than just selling the Shopify holding, paying capital gains tax upfront and investing in a stock index fund.
  • Wash sale constraints reduce harvesting in practice. We assumed daily tax-loss harvesting as if the wash sale rule didn’t bind – i.e., that every loss could be realized and the same stock repurchased immediately. This maximizes the amount of tax-loss harvesting achievable. However, in the real world, the wash sale constraint, together with limits on acceptable tracking error between the long and short portfolios, considerably reduce the losses that can be harvested. If we delay harvesting on 20% of losses each year, the expected after-tax return of the LSDI program in our example drops by 0.29% pa.
  • The investor bears more risk under LSDI. The LSDI portfolio is riskier than the immediate-sale alternative for two reasons: the investor retains exposure to her concentrated Shopify holding for longer,9 and the long/short portfolio itself adds risk. We calculate that over the 20-year period, she’ll experience about 3 percentage points higher portfolio volatility than in the immediate-sale case, ignoring any risk arising from the long/short portfolio of individual stocks. This extra risk reduces her expected compound return, and for a typically risk-averse investor, the cost of bearing that risk goes beyond its drag on compounding.
  • We ignored transaction costs. Bid-ask spreads, commissions, market impact, and predatory trading against a predictable rebalancing pattern all take a toll on the LSDI program’s heavy trading. With our base case assumptions, we estimate average portfolio turnover of around 400% annually on the net value of the portfolio of the LSDI program over the 20-year horizon.
  • At most other horizons LSDI is worse, particularly shorter ones. For example, if we moved the horizon to five years from 20 years, LSDI becomes more than 2% pa less attractive relative to the sell-and-move-on approach. This primarily arises from paying the higher tax rate (17% higher) on short-term capital gains arising from covering the LSDI short positions at the horizon.
  • Tax rates and rules may adversely change. Many economists and tax experts believe tax rates are more likely to rise than fall, and that favorable provisions like step-up in basis risk being curtailed. Any such changes would further erode the case for LSDI.
  • Difficult to implement for non-public stock-holdings. Many potential use cases involve investors who own stakes in non-public companies (we hear that employees of SpaceX, Stripe, Anthropic, OpenAI, and Databricks are hearing a lot about LSDI these days) and it is difficult for them to get the leverage needed to implement the strategy as described above. Slower-moving programs that start with smaller amounts of LSDI as the investor starts to sell and diversify away from the concentrated holding in secondary-market transactions have less attractive economics than the case we set out above.
  • Better alternatives to an immediate sale exist. For example, we assumed that the investor sold her appreciated asset and bought and held an index fund. In practice, rotating among similar index ETFs could provide effectively free tax-loss harvesting opportunities.

When LSDI can make sense

There are circumstances in which LSDI may be a reasonable choice, though none of the below considerations on their own will be conclusive:

  • You expect the manager to generate significant alpha. If you believe the manager of the program can consistently and materially beat the market through stock selection, the calculus changes. A recently published article from researchers at AQR notes that “Investors should not be drawn to these strategies solely because of their tax benefits. Instead, they should evaluate them as they would any other risky investment and avoid those lacking a defensible source of pre-tax alpha.” We agree with AQR, and our analysis quantifies just how large that alpha needs to be, depending on your particular circumstances.
  • You’re moving from a high-tax to a low-tax state. If you plan to relocate in the near future, the temporary tax deferral may have significant value. We model the scenario of moving from California to Florida, and with a ten-year horizon example, LSDI comes out 0.9% pa ahead of the immediate sale case.
  • You expect step-up in basis and soon. If you expect to pass away within the next ten years or so, and believe your appreciated long-side assets will receive a step-up in basis at death, LSDI has an expected compound return 0.44% pa higher than the immediate-sale case. can be a material benefit adding about 1% pa of after-tax return, but to longer horizons of 20 or 30 years, step-up in basis adds about 0.15% and 0.07% pa of return to the LSDI case, respectively, hardly changing the overall picture. Note that short positions do no get stepped up.
  • Charitable intentions. There may be cases where the interaction with intentions to donate appreciated assets to charity may warrant engaging in an LSDI program. However, it’s often superior to make donations sooner, rather than saving them for offsetting gains down the road. We also note that directly donating a short position to a charity is not allowed.
  • Hedge fund investors. For example, you invest in hedge funds which give you short-term capital gains each year, and you want to use an LSDI program to convert short-term capital gains to long-term capital gains. We have not analyzed such a case, but it seems plausible that an LSDI program produces benefits after fees, but that will very much depend on the particular circumstances and assumptions.
  • More complex structured variations. We’ve heard of some investors wrapping LSDI in complex structures. We don’t know much about them, but we suspect they are not available or cost-efficient unless the size of the program involves $50 million or more of gains.
  • You’d rather pay a wealth manager than the IRS. This is a personal preference, but it’s worth being explicit about it.

Connecting the dots

We think it’s important for investors to focus on returns after-tax, after-fee, after-inflation, and after-cost-of-risk. But sometimes we worry that investors become so focused on minimizing taxes that they lose sight of this overall objective. Long/short direct index tax-loss harvesting may be a case where the promise of paying no taxes “as far as the eye can see” short-circuits a more holistic analysis – one that also accounts for fees, other costs, risk, and a realistic assessment of the odds of beating the market through stock picking.

An immediate sale of an appreciated asset and reinvestment in index funds is not the only alternative to an LSDI program, and unlikely to be the optimal action to take with a highly appreciated, concentrated holding. Other approaches exist and may do the job better than either of the two alternatives discussed in this note.

If you’re being pitched or exploring an LSDI program, we recommend asking the manager to show you results with no pre-tax alpha and ask them to use our assumptions to see if they roughly match our output, including not just the central outcome but the variability around it.

This is admittedly a more personal observation, but we also wonder: if given the choice, would more people on reflection prefer to contribute $100 in taxes – incrementing the common resources of their state and nation – than to pay $100 in fees to a wealth manager, no matter how sweet, intelligent and friendly we are?

Rather than reporting on LSDI being part of a “$1 trillion business…a new gold rush… helping wealthy Americans crush their tax bills,”10 we hope the next Bloomberg or WSJ headline will tell a more complete story: the only party that is with near-certainty better off is Wall Street, while wealthy investors and the government, both individually and together, are likely worse off.

Appendix

Simulation results are available in this Google sheet.

Simulation overview

Our simulation model starts with an index of 200 names, with a Pareto distribution of market-cap weights calibrated to match the S&P500. Each name follows geometric brownian motion (GBM) with a fixed expected return (6% arithmetic), fixed volatility (30%), and fixed pairwise correlation (0.3), resulting in a volatility of about 17% for the index. The simulation is run on 2000 daily paths.

The long ‘reinvestment’ portfolio invests in the index, and the long/short portfolio invests in the index on both sides – so the net PnL of the long/short portfolio is always zero, before fees and transaction costs. We also assume no wash sale rule, so positions with losses can be sold and immediately replaced. These are obviously unrealistic assumptions, but in the direction of being generous to the LSDI strategy – the simulation results should reflect an upper bound on the amount and value of harvesting available.

Harvested losses from the reinvestment portfolio and the long/short portfolio are used to absorb the embedded gain in an existing appreciated asset. The simulation runs in two phases – first we use realized losses to gradually sell the asset without realizing gains, then losses are used to partially unwind the long/short book pro-rata, also without realizing gains.

PhasesThe LSDI simulation runs in two sequential phases, plus a final horizon settlement.

Phase 1 – Sell-down.The goal of this phase is to move out of the appreciated asset entirely without paying tax. Daily loss harvesting runs on the reinvestment portfolio and the long/short book, building up ST and LT loss pools. At each year-end, the model computes the maximum number of asset units that can be sold such that the realized LT gain from the sale is fully absorbed by the available loss pools. Those units are sold, the corresponding losses are consumed from the pools, and the cash proceeds are immediately reinvested in the index as a new tranche. That new tranche joins the daily harvesting loop for the remainder of the simulation, generating additional losses going forward. Phase 1 continues year after year until either the appreciated asset is fully exhausted or the simulation reaches the horizon.

Phase 2 – Wind-down.Once Phase 1 ends, the goal shifts to unwinding the long/short book itself – closing long and short positions without realizing net gains. Daily harvesting still runs on the reinvestment and long/short portfolios. At each year-end, we solve for the largest fraction f such that closing f of every remaining long and short position produces zero net tax, given the current loss pools and the composition of the unrealized gains. That fraction is closed across all active positions, and the loss pools are drawn down accordingly. The wind-down repeats annually; each year’s harvesting restocks the pools, enabling another close-out slice. The portfolio is thus progressively drained until either it’s fully closed or the horizon arrives.

HorizonAt the horizon date, we do the following:

  1. Remaining appreciated asset (if any): taxed on its full embedded LT gain at the terminal LT rate (0% if step_up = True).
  2. Reinvestment portfolio (tranches): not taxed – the modeling assumption is that these positions receive their own step-up or are held indefinitely past horizon. Their unrealized gains are reported as an output but don’t hit the terminal tax bill.
  3. L/S book (if not fully wound down): long unrealized gains taxed at the terminal LT rate (0 if step_up), short unrealized gains taxed at the terminal ST rate (never stepped up – shorts don’t get step-up treatment). The long/short net market value is ≈ 0 by construction (market-neutral), so only the tax on the unrealized side matters.
  4. CA/FL relocation: if the state is CA/FL, the terminal settlement uses non-CA rates (the investor has moved to FL by horizon), while the immediate-sale portfolio uses CA rates.

Portfolios

Reference portfolio: At t = 0, the appreciated asset is sold outright; the investor pays LT tax on the embedded gain. There’s no harvesting, no fees, no turnover. This is the counterfactual against which LSDI IRR is measured.

LSDI – four moving pieces:

  1. Appreciated asset: Assumed to have zero basis, and follows its own correlated GBM path with the same expected return as each stock in the index. It is held intact until Phase 1 year-ends, when units are gradually sold – each year only as many units as can have their realized LT gain fully offset by available ST+LT loss pools. Sale proceeds fund the reinvestment portfolio. Any residual asset still held at horizon is marked to market and taxed at the terminal LT rate (0% if step_up).
  2. Reinvestment portfolio (tranches): Each annual asset sale purchases a new tranche. Tranches are bought at the current stock prices using the same Pareto weight template, and each tranche thereafter participates in daily loss harvesting on its own basis (so each tranche has its own ST/LT clock and its own unrealized P&L). The reinvestment portfolio is the sum of all live tranches. It is not taxed at the horizon – the implicit assumption is that tranches receive a step-up or are held indefinitely past horizon. Its unrealized gains are reported but don’t hit the terminal tax line.
  3. Long-side L/S portfolio: The long index portfolio which is harvested daily as available, whatever’s left from the wind-down phase is unwound at horizon at the prevailing LT rate (0 if step_up).
  4. Short-side L/S portfolio: The short index portfolio which is harvested daily as available, whatever’s left from the wind-down phase is unwound at horizon at the prevailing ST rate.

Further Reading and References

  • Haghani, V. and White, J. (2025).“Out of the Frying Pan and Into the Fire: Selling A Highly Appreciated Stock Without Paying Taxes?” SSRN.
  • Haghani, V. and White, J. (2024). “Direct Indexed Tax Loss Harvesting: Is the Juice Worth the Squeeze?” Elm Wealth.
  • Haghani, V. and White, J. (2020). “To Realize, or Not to Realize.” Elm Wealth.
  • Haghani, V. and White, J. (2023). “Chapter 17: Tax Matters.” The Missing Billionaires: A Guide to Better Financial Decisions. Wiley.
  • Haghani, V. and White, J. (2017). “How Much Should the Tax Tail Wag the Asset Allocation Dog?” Elm Wealth.
  • Haghani, V. and White, J. (2018). “US Tax Reform Leaves Even Less of the Pie for Individual Investors in Alternatives.” Elm Wealth.
  • Haghani, V., Hilibrand, L. and White, J. (2019). “When it Pays to Pay Capital Gains.” Elm Wealth.
  • Liberman, J., Sosner, N. and Freitas, P. (March 2026). “The Tax Benefits of Pre-Tax Alpha.” SSRN.
  • Liberman, J., Sialm C., Sosner, N. and Wang, L. (2020). “The Tax Benefits of Separating Alpha from Beta.” Financial Analysts Journal 76:1.
  • Moehle, N., Kochenderfer, M., Boyd, S., and Ang, A. (2021). “Tax-Aware Portfolio Construction via Convex Optimization.” BlackRock AI Labs.
  • Sialm, C. and Sosner, N. (2018). “Taxes, Shorting, and Active Management.” Financial Analysts Journal 74:1.
  • Quantitative Investment Strategies. (Feb 2018). “The Benefits of Tax Loss Harvesting Strategies in Investment Portfolios.” Goldman Sachs Asset Management.
  1. Such as this one from Bloomberg: “Slashing Tax Bills for Rich Investors Is a $1 Trillion Business” by Justina Lee and Denitsa Tsekova (March 16, 2026).
  2. BlackRock. “Strategies for Managing a Concentrated Stock Position.” (2024).
  3. We chose Shopify for this example to liven things up a bit versus the typical use of Apple and Nvidia for these examples.
  4. See Section 1091(e) of the Internal Revenue Code. The wash sale rule imposes a 31-day window before or after a loss realization during which you cannot replace your position with a “substantially identical” security.
  5. In more technical terms, 6% is the arithmetic expected return, net of dividends.
  6. Shopify’s annualized volatility of daily returns over the past two years was 58%. Many other high-flying companies which might have become concentrated public market single stock positions – such as Broadcom, Crowdstrike, Tesla, Palantir, Coinbase, Datadog, Twilio and Pinterest, to name a few – have realized volatility of over 50% over the past two years.
  7. We believe these are typical fees. However, we have recently heard that Fidelity and Schwab are no longer offering SMA custody for new LSDI programs, or if they do, that they are charging a long/short fee in excess of the 0.75% we used for our analysis.
  8. We don’t actually present value the fees in the analysis – they’re just counted as part of the cash-flows when calculating the IRRs, but if you want to get a sense for what the fees are in present value terms, they’re about $1.9 million discounted at 4%.
  9. This risk can be partly mitigated by structuring the long/short portfolio to account for the single stock holding risk – which some LSDI programs try to do – but for most single stocks, it’s challenging to reduce the single stock idiosyncratic risk significantly.
  10. Justina Lee and Denitsa Tsekova, “Slashing Tax Bills for Rich Investors Is a $1 Trillion Business,” Bloomberg, March 2026.

data.title: The Best Choice When It Comes To Two Harbors Preferred Equity
data.description:
Thesis

Two Harbors Investment Corp. (TWO) is a mortgage REIT out of Minnesota that has gone through a virtual corporate drama in the past few months. A simple corporate take-over turned sour, only to have other suitors appear and snatch TWO from its original merger agreement. No drama is complete without dramatic 'break-up' messages from the 'ex' (i.e. original suitor):

“The actions by TWO’s management and board do not reflect the best interests of their shareholders. The same team that had to settle a $375 million lawsuit this past summer is at it again. TWO’s decision appears to be driven more by ego, than by sound judgment," the company said in a statement. "The deal for us was a strategy to acquire their servicing book, not their operations, as ultimately there are no operational efficiencies to gain — UWMs operations are best in class," it said.

In today's article we are going to go through the time-line of the above corporate drama, the current situation and the implications to the REIT's capital structure. In the end we will show readers why the Series C preferred equity is the best option currently from TWO.

Timeline of acquisition

Episode 1: Love at first sight

The entire corporate drama started in December 2025, when UWMC, a large mortgage provider, proposed to acquire TWO:

PONTIAC, Mich. & NEW YORK--(BUSINESS WIRE)--UWM Holdings Corporation (“UWMC”) and Two Harbors Investment Corp. (“TWO”) (NYSE: TWO), today announced that they have entered into a definitive merger agreement pursuant to which UWMC will acquire TWO in an all-stock transaction for $1.3 billion in equity value, based on a fixed exchange ratio of 2.3328x. The transaction has been unanimously approved by the Boards of Directors of both UWMC and TWO. The transaction is expected to close in the second quarter of 2026, subject to approval of TWO’s stockholders and the satisfaction of other closing conditions, including customary regulatory approvals.

The proposed merger though would be an equity for equity one, meaning TWO common holders would get UWMC equity rather than outright cash or a mix of cash and equity. Now, as we can see from the above, TWO holders would get a fixed amount of UWMC shares upon the merger's completion, thus being fully subject to the equity fluctuations of the UWMC stock. Basically, the selling point for the TWO common shareholders was that their equity stake was set to appreciate in value as part of a larger, more diverse company like UWMC.

As per the announcement, while the boards of directors approved the merger, it was still subject to a common shareholders' vote. If the TWO common shareholders do not vote in favor, the merger does not happen.

Episode 2: The relationship becomes rocky

While the board of directors for TWO had approved the merger, the common holders for TWO had to do the same. And this was slated to happen in March 2026. However, the relationship started to become rocky as the overall markets sold off and the UWMC stock tanked:

Since early January 2026 we saw an almost -50% drawdown in the UWMC stock, which meant the value for TWO shareholders became smaller and smaller. Since TWO contains many MSR rights, its book value remained fairly resilient through the market sell-off thus creating deep dissatisfaction with the UWMC offer. An all-cash bid from UWMC would have avoided this issue, but for internal reasons they wanted to have the acquisition done via equity only.

One of the potential reasons for an all-equity take-over is the fact that UWMC owner Mat Ishbia keeps selling UWMC equity:

This is not an isolated event, with Ishbia having dumped significant amounts of stock for months now. So the market knew there was selling pressure in UWMC and sold short UWMC after the merger announcement. Even today, UWMC has a very high short percentage of 13%:

Episode 3: Other suitors appear

As the relationship deteriorated as the UWMC common stock dumped, we saw an interesting development where the TWO common equity de-coupled from the UWMC common equity. This simply meant that the market no longer believed a merger was going to occur.

TWO's business model was still appealing (even more so after the public announcements of the potential synergies with UWMC), and other suitors appeared:

Two Harbors Investment (TWO) stock surged 11% in Thursday premarket trading after it received an unsolicited proposal for $10.70 per share in cash, the company said on Thursday. That comes after the mortgage REIT had already agreed to be acquired by mortgage wholesaler UWM Holdings (UWMC) in an all-stock deal that valued each share of TWO at $11.94 on Dec. 16, 2025. At the close of March 18, the UWMC deal valued each share of TWO at ~$8.61.

To note the new offer was the opposite of the original UWMC one because it was an all-cash proposal that took away the issues associated with the decrease in value for the UWMC stock.

Episode 4: The break-up, and the happy ever after

Given the better value proposition from the new bid, TWO gave UWMC a chance to improve their proposal, but to no avail. We are of the opinion that if UWMC had sweetened its offer to be at least 50%-60% cash, the original merger agreement would have gone through. But UWMC did not actually change its bid in any shape or form, prompting the TWO board to accept the new merger proposal:

Two Harbors Investment (TWO) on Thursday announced a new definitive merger agreement with CrossCountry Mortgage, terminating its previously announced agreement with UWM Holdings (UWMC). Last week, Two said it received an unsolicited proposal for $10.70 per share in cash. The new proposal provides for the payment of the $25.4M termination fee that would be required under the UWMC agreement. Earlier this week, Two board's ad hoc committee said that CrossCountry Mortgage's unsolicited bid constitutes a "superior" proposal to the merger agreement it had previously reached with UWM Holdings.

CrossCountry Mortgage (to be referred to as 'CCM') is a private mortgage lender out of Ohio. By virtue of being private, the company cannot offer its equity thus the all cash offer. Although private, CCM does have traded debt outstanding and holds a BB- rating from Fitch:

While the TWO board of directors has approved the CCM bid, the TWO common shareholders have not done that yet, with a vote set to occur in early May 2026. We are of the opinion the vote will go through since the bid is all cash and superior to prior offers.

Impact to the TWO capital structure

The impact to the common equity is fairly clear, with the shares moving very close to the bid level, signaling a near-certainity of the merger happening:

The new company will assume all the TWO debt and repos, and will adjust them as they see fit. Now the more interesting question resides with the TWO preferred equity which we hold. CCM actually addressed that in their merger announcement:

Under the terms of the agreement, TWO stockholders will receive $10.80 in cash for each share of TWO common stock. Holders of TWO’s Series A, Series B and Series C Preferred Stock will have their shares redeemed following the closing of the transaction at $25.00 per share, plus any accumulated and unpaid dividends, in accordance with the terms of the preferred stock.

So CCM intends to redeem the preferred stock once the merger concludes from a legal stand-point. This is great news for the preferred holders, with all the series rallying hard on the news. The current yields on the preferred shares are as follows:

  • Series A (TWO.PR.A): 8.2%
  • Series B (TWO.PR.B): 7.7%
  • Series C (TWO.PR.C): 9.0%

Series C has the highest current yield because it is floating rate, re-setting at 3m SOFR + 0.261% + 5%. With the Iran war ongoing SOFR will stay stable for the next few months, thus ensuring a high coupon. The Series A and Series B have their first call dates in 2027, thus will remain at fixed coupons until then.

Since the CCM merger acts like a maturity date for the preferred shares, we can also look at price and try to derive a yield to maturity:

  • Series A: $24.81/share
  • Series B: $24.77/share
  • Series C: $24.88/share

For the CCM merger to be complete the TWO shareholders need to approve it (in the next month it will happen) and then regulatory approvals need to be obtained. That puts the completion date somewhere in the second half of 2026, thus September 30, 2026 is a good guess as of now. That leaves roughly two payment dates for the preferred shares - one in July 2026 and one coming in October 2026 (the shares will be retired with accrued interest). Calculating yield to maturities to a hypothetical September 30 2026 closing date gives us the following:

  • Series A: 9.9% YTM
  • Series B: 9.7% YTM
  • Series C: 10% YTM

The Series C therefore has the highest current yield and the highest yield to maturity, thus representing the best way to take advantage of the merger. Given the high probability of the merger going through, we anticipate very little movement in the market price of the securities, thus making them an appealing short term trade.

Conclusion

TWO lived through the drama of a failed acquisition, only to be pursued by a different suitor and offered an all-cash deal. The beneficiaries here are not only the common shareholders, but also the preferred equity which is going to be taken out at par once the merger goes through. TWO.PR.C is the most attractive series of preferred equity with the highest current yield and yield to maturity. We hold TWO.PR.C and will buy some more as a short term trade that yields 10% to the expected September 2026 maturity.

data.title: Valero: Windfall Profits Lead To 14% Capital Returns
data.description:
Introduction

When the Iran war turned from a weekend event to a credible threat to global energy markets, I invested in Valero Energy (VLO) as a hedge (along with Venture Global (VG), Core Natural Resources (CNR), Vista Energy (VIST), Gran Tierra (GTE), and Petrobras (PBR)), and now, as the war drags on, with the oil & gas flows likely to provoke fuel shortages, refiners with access to WTI may earn above-average margins for longer. Add to this the structural deficit in refining capacity vs. fuel demand, and Valero could see a reset in crack spreads that allows it to pay nearly 14% in dividends and share buybacks.

Refining Pricing Power

There are several reasons US refiners have enjoyed higher spreads during 2025 and should see them increase in 2026.

The first is due to a structural deficit in US refining capacity that has been declining for 5 years, which requires imports that drive wholesale prices higher. This price shock is evident when the Russian supply was sanctioned and now with the Gulf supply trapped.

A second factor is the configuration of refining capacity, which was built to process heavier crude oil from Mexico/Venezuela rather than light oil produced in the shale areas. Refiners import heavy oil at a discount but can price it at a premium vs. WTI.

Finally, demand for gas/diesel and most refined products is still increasing, while refining capacity is not, which gradually shifts pricing power to the refiner who fills domestic demand rather than the oil producer who sells into a global benchmark price.

These structural “problems” should persist; it's unlikely new refining capacity will be built in the US or that existing capacity will be modified to refine light oil.

Valero Operating Model

While oil refining is a capital-intensive and complex technical operation, the business side can be viewed as simpler. The refiner buys crude oil, processes it into as many as seven products, and sells them at wholesale prices to distribution companies or end users. The key is to buy low-cost oil, maintain efficient process operations, and sell the end product at a higher price. However, given the commodity nature of the business, where product differentiation is low or nonexistent, achieving a good margin or crack spread depends more on market forces and the configuration of supply/demand dynamics. As mentioned earlier, this currently favors refiners.

The operating model below illustrates the relationship between the refined sales price and oil input costs relative to the benchmark WTI. Valero sold its BOEs (barrels of oil equivalent), which include all refined products, at about 72% above WTI in 2025, at $111 BOE, while it acquired those BOEs and other chemicals, plus transport, etc., to process the oil at around $92 BOE, or 42% above WTI. The difference is the refining margin, or crack spread. This accounts for 80% of EBITDA, with SGA and other operating expenses at 6% of revenue and excise taxes at 5% of revenue (mostly for its offshore operations).

In 2022, due to the Ukraine invasion energy shock, WTI increased 35% to average $94.7, while Valero’s average sales price grew 46%, and input costs increased 39%, which netted a gain of $12.6 per BOE, or double the 2021 crack spread, and drove EBITDA to $18bn.

The current market environment is, in my view, far more complex, with a greater portion of global oil and refined products offline and trapped or, in some cases, destroyed or damaged. The WTI/Brent futures are not accurate prices for physical delivery of crude or refined products. This means that Valero may see windfall profits as it sells its refined BOEs at a higher premium. I believe it can garner an upward of a 76% premium to WTI and earn nearly a $28 BOE spread in 2026. While the margin may be similar to 2025 at 18%, the higher absolute price and cost dilution from OPEX can increase the EBITDA margin to 9%, leading to nearly double EBITDA.

While the Iran war and blockade may be resolved in a few weeks or months, normalization of the energy supply chain will take most of 2026, with a spillover into 2027, and Valero should continue to generate over $10bn in EBITDA through 2028.

1Q26 Results Preview

Given that the Iran war and oil shock began in March, the company may see some impact on revenue and margins, but the real driver will be the updated guidance that should provide the market with a view into how this oil supply shock could boost earnings and what they plan to do with this windfall, i.e., dividends. The consensus estimates EBITDA at $2.1bn, which is more than double the 1Q25 results when spreads were very tight. Consensus EBITDA for 2Q26 is currently over $3.4bn, an entirely new level and what the market should be focusing on.

Valuation

Given that Valero is a cyclic commodity business, valuation is not set vs. growth but at a fixed multiple that incorporates the ups and downs of its business, much like an oil and mining company. The market price target, based on still-low consensus estimates, is $250, or a 7.3x EV/EBITDA multiple for 2026.

I believe the stock should be valued as a bond proxy at a considerable discount to corporate investment-grade bonds (5%). Based on my estimates, Valero could return 14% in dividends and buybacks at current prices, and to reach a 10% yield, the price would need to increase to the $280 level, which is my price target.

Note that the company does not have significant expansion plans, and capex is under depreciation levels. It's also underleveraged at less than 0.8x net debt/EBITDA (ND/EBITDA) and therefore should pay out nearly all its FCF. The low debt levels should be maintained to fund working capital and provide some tax benefits.

Peer Comps

I gathered consensus data on pure refining companies (excluding Reliance Industries, an Indian conglomerate) to measure Valero against growth, margins, and valuations. As can be seen in the table below, the sector is set to have a banner year, with EPS growth of over 80%, which may still be conservative given the ongoing energy blockade. However, this is not a growth sector, and valuation multiples should not expand; capital gains may be temporary unless accompanied by high capital returns. Valor has lower leverage vs. Marathon Petroleum (MPC) and Phillips 66 (PSX), which may allow for more aggressive capital returns.

Risk

The main risk is the destruction of demand for oil and refined products. Which means a sharp global recession. Valero and most refiners would lose pricing power and could see margins collapse. Oil substitution, a dramatic increase in EVs (cars and trucks) in the US and globally, may also dent demand and weaken margins. The building of new refining capacity in the US would also signal a more competitive market and impact valuations before actual results. Windfall profits taxes that the US government could impose to smooth transportation costs.

Conclusion

I rate Valero a Buy. The Iran war has disrupted global energy flows and will likely lead to refined product shortages, which will provide refiners like Valero with pricing power, high crack spreads, and windfall profits. The normalization of supply may require 6 to 12 months after hostilities end, keeping spreads high into 2027. In addition, it's unlikely refining capacity will be added in the US, and refiners may maintain premium pricing as long as domestic fuel demand continues to outpace domestic refining output. However, Valero is not a growth stock, and much of its value to investors comes from its ability to return capital (dividends and buybacks). I estimate a 14% yield that, when priced at a discount to investment-grade bonds, can provide investors with double-digit total returns for several years.

data.title: United Natural Foods: The Gains Should Continue, But Slow
data.description:
From one perspective, the rally in United Natural Foods, Inc. (UNFI) stock is stunning. In just under two years, shares have gained 471%—seemingly a huge rally for a food distributor with thin margins and relatively low top-line growth.

From another, however, the rally is not surprising at all. UNFI clearly had the potential for such a move, in part because in barely a year the stock had fallen ~80% into those lows. And in part because margins are so thin (and in part because of a leveraged balance sheet, which raised fear on the way down and provided a potential catalyst on the way up), it didn't take that much of a change for the stock to move.

In April 2024, the midpoint of full-year guidance for fiscal 2024 (ending early August) called for $30.8 billion in revenue and adjusted EBITDA margins of 1.62%. Two years later, the midpoint sits at $31.2 billion in revenue (up barely 1%) and margins of 2.23%.

Essentially, 61 basis points of margin expansion have created 400%-plus gains (and well over $2 billion in added market capitalization). The margin-driven pathway to those gains existed on paper before this rally, certainly; United Natural Foods simply wasn't able to actually create that expansion in practice, and quite clearly the market two years ago didn't believe that would change.

Now that it has, the story here remains interesting but, quite obviously, different. UNFI management has (rightly) earned the market's trust. Further expansion does suggest continued upside, albeit perhaps not at the rate seen over the past 24 months. And in a market where, over and over again, triple-digit rallies have kept going (and going and going and going...), there's at worst a solid case for UNFI here.

The Simple Case For UNFI Stock

Even with the huge rally in UNFI over the past 24 months, the bull case here remains relatively simple to make. Again, selling a stock because "it has run too far" has been a huge error in this market, and taking a broader view, it's not hard to see that even that ostensible reason for bearishness starts to fade away:

It's not as if valuation is questionable. Based on trailing twelve-month figures coming out of fiscal Q2 earnings, UNFI trades at under 8x on an enterprise value to EBITDA basis. Balance sheet guidance for the end of FY26 drops that multiple below 7x. On a full-year guided basis, price to free cash flow is under 10x; the normalized multiple is probably a touch higher but still completely reasonable.

Operationally, management looks impressive. Chief executive officer Sandy Douglas did oversee the decline into 2024 lows, but the work done then, particularly around 'lean' operations, seems to have paid off. As detailed at a December Investor Day, shrink is down over the past two years, and execution is improving. The 61 bps in margin seem driven pretty much solely by overall operational improvements, and in the context of a $30 billion-plus revenue basis, those improvements quite obviously have real value.

Financially, better results have also led to a much-improved balance sheet. UNFI was starting to look unwieldy at the lows, with net leverage heading toward 4x Adjusted EBITDA. Management now is targeting a year-end ratio of 2.3x, putting the company on much firmer footing.

And the external environment looks positive as well. As management has noted, the natural/organic side of the grocery business is growing faster than the industry as a whole. UNFI sees a roughly $90 billion total addressable market (which it, somewhat incredibly, trimmed last year; I can't personally ever remember a management team lowering its TAM estimate), with roughly two-thirds of that on the organic side. Like so many consumer-facing industries, there is a bit of an "hourglass effect" in grocery. The biggest, lowest-price champions (Walmart (WMT) and Costco (COST) most obviously) are winning on one side, and, as chief financial officer Giorgio Tarditi put it at a conference last month, "differentiated retailers", including natural/organic, are winning on the other. UNFI has strong exposure to the latter group, particularly given its large contract with Amazon (AMZN) unit Whole Foods, which in 2024 was extended to 2032.

Valuing United Natural Foods Stock

Given a management target of ~$800 million in adjusted EBITDA in fiscal 2028, there's a case for significant upside from here. A 6.5x multiple (more on this in a moment) to that number would put EV at $5.2 billion; assuming net debt drops to $1.3 billion thanks to free cash flow, the implied market cap of $3.9 billion would move the stock roughly 30% higher.

There's likely upside to all of those inputs. Bulls, for instance, might argue that 6.5x is far too conservative. Particularly since the 'lean' improvements were implemented, management has generally outperformed its targets. There's more room for margin expansion on that front (UNFI is targeting another ~65 bps by fiscal 2028), and an ERP (enterprise resource planning) implementation may offer further help. Generative artificial intelligence is another potential avenue toward cutting costs and boosting profits.

Given how thin margins are here, it doesn't take that much for UNFI to materially beat market expectations. Indeed, that's precisely how the stock has gained 400%-plus over the last 24 months. And, fundamentally, there is a strong argument that against the base case scenario, which still offers ~14% annualized returns, United Natural Foods is more likely to outperform than underperform.

What Goes Wrong Here

Of course, there are risks. And from here, there are three primary concerns.

The first is, perhaps surprisingly, around valuation. A 6.5x multiple in this model doesn't sound like much, but it's not guaranteed UNFI can get much more than that. SpartanNash (which admittedly plays mostly in a different part of the distribution market) went private at about 6x EBITDA. Other peer comparisons aren't particularly helpful (the two main competitors UNFI faces, C&S and KeHe Distributors, are both private), but thin margins and relatively low returns on invested capital do suggest something of a lid on valuation.

Indeed, UNFI's historical range suggests the current valuation might be about where the stock settles (note that the chart includes operating leases in enterprise value):

And if multiples are capped in the ~6x EBITDA / ~10x free cash flow range here, there is a question of what comes next. FY28 targets do suggest impressive margin expansion, but at a certain point the low-hanging fruit gets harvested. As Douglas noted at the Investor Day, "We brought lean practices into a business that had not yet benefited from the discipline that this practice brings." The obvious concern is that once those benefits arrive—and by FY28 it does seem like most, if not all, will have done so—margin expansion starts to stall out, and bottom-line growth does the same. (Even UNFI itself only expects low-single-digit sales growth annually.)

This all coincides with an external environment that is perhaps more favorable than usual. Management has noted tailwinds behind at-home versus away-from-home spending amid inflation concerns (the latter itself a tailwind to margins). So there may be something of a cyclical effect to recent improvements as well—one that will inevitably reverse, as it always does.

Finally, there's the fact that for UNFI, historically something always has gone wrong:

There simply have been a number of points over UNFI's three decades on the public market at which the company seemed set for consistent success. At most of those points, something has gone wrong. Total returns since the initial public offering admittedly aren't terrible (600 percent-plus, or nearly 7% annualized), but they have lagged the index, with obviously a tremendous amount of volatility on the way.

To be sure, every stock has risks, and the concerns for UNFI overall seem to point more to appreciation slowing rather than reversing. There is at the least a solid case for the stock, and it's possible that this time simply is different. If the company doesn't get tripped up again, there is obviously plenty of room for more upside here.

data.title: Enbridge: A Downgrade After A Great Run
data.description:
Back in July 2025, I decided to reaffirm Enbridge (ENB) as a "Buy" candidate. The company had achieved solid growth and looked priced at levels that were attractive to me. The secured capital projects that the business had under its belt paved the way for additional expansion. Ultimately, my decision to reaffirm it as a "Buy" candidate has played out nicely. The stock is up 27.3% since then, while the S&P 500 is up only 8.4%. But since I originally turned bullish about the business in July 2018, shares have seen an upside of 152.9%. That's not drastically higher than the 145% increase that the market experienced. But it is higher all the same.

Looking at the picture now, I see a firm that, compared to other players in the space, is decent, but not great. In light of this, I actually would argue that downgrading it to a "Hold" likely makes sense here. Of course, my opinion on the matter could change as new data comes in. On May 8th, management is expected to announce financial results for the first quarter of the company's 2026 fiscal year. But unless management really knocks it out of the park, I doubt that upgrading it back to a "Buy" will make sense.

A necessary disclosure

Before I dive in, there is one disclosure that I need to make here. Because it is a Canadian company, Enbridge reports its financial results in Canadian dollars as opposed to the U.S. dollar. Since I think of things in U.S. dollar terms and since I imagine most of my readers do as well, I have converted all financial performance figures for the company into U.S. dollars at the current exchange rate. However, all presentation materials from the business will still be in Canadian dollars.

Time for a downgrade

Fundamentally speaking, Enbridge has a history of rather attractive growth. In the chart above, for instance, you can see financial performance figures for the last three completed fiscal years. Revenue, profits, and most cash flow metrics have increased during this time. There are two data points that might seem peculiar to you. The first of these is adjusted operating cash flow. This is essentially operating cash flow after stripping out changes in working capital. And then the other one is what I refer to as "true free cash flow." I take the adjusted operating cash flow figure for the company and strip out from it maintenance capital spending. This allows me to see what the free cash flow picture of the business looks like on a normalized basis without punishing it for its investments in growth opportunities. And it is, I would argue, the single most important profitability or cash flow metric for midstream/pipeline enterprises. But I digress.

According to management, the company has seen significant growth in a couple of areas. Take, if you will, the Gas and Distribution segment of the business. Over the last three years, segment EBITDA for this unit surged 139.3%. This is a significant operation for the company, consisting of gas distribution assets spread across several states such as Ohio, North Carolina, Utah, Wyoming, and Idaho. It also has assets in Ontario and Quebec in Canada. Not to mention, it also involves the firm's ownership in Wexpro Company, which focuses on developing and producing natural gas reserves across three different states. While the company has been investing in organic growth here, it is important to note that, across 2023 and 2024, the business acquired significant assets involving these operations. Most notably, the business acquired three U.S. natural gas utilities from Dominion Energy (D) in transactions worth about $14 billion in total.

This has not been the only area of expansion for the business. Segment EBITDA associated with its Renewable Power Generation segment grew an astounding 316.1% over the course of three years. All of that, and more, occurred from 2023 to 2024, with this largely the result of the absence of a large impairment charge in 2023 and the benefit of certain investments that the company made, such as its investment in Fox Squirrel Solar, and contributions from Hohe See and Albatros Offshore Wind.

One of the great things about the company is that management continues to focus on ways to grow the business. In 2025 alone, for instance, the company commissioned CAD$14 billion, or approximately $10.10 billion, in projects that will collectively support more than 7GW of power generation. But that's just the beginning. Management believes that there exist around CAD$50 billion (or $36.08 billion) of opportunities through the year 2030. This will come as North American gas demand will jump by 23Bcf per day between 2025 and the end of the decade, while liquids pipelines will benefit from around 1MMbpd of increased production.

We should see an annualized growth rate through 2029 when it comes to US utility rates, and the renewable power market should continue to grow, with a 19% increase in renewables demand from 2020 through 2030. I am personally very excited about the renewables side of the business. The company is already working with multiple companies, such as Facebook parent Meta Platforms (META), to advance more than 1GW of new development opportunities by servicing customers such as Meta Platforms, Amazon (AMZN), and AT&T (T). This includes a major solar generation facility worth about $1.2 billion and a renewable power development facility in Texas worth about $0.4 billion. There's also the opportunity for growth in Europe, including through a 2.5GW portfolio of government support and offshore wind assets.

When it comes to this year specifically, management has high expectations. In the image above, you can see the thoughts that management has when it comes to overall profitability. Using the current exchange rate, and the midpoint of expectations, we are looking at EBITDA this year of about $14.79 billion. DCF, or distributable cash flow, should be somewhere around $9.31 billion. And if we scale up other profitability metrics at a similar rate, we would be looking at adjusted operating cash flow of approximately $10.14 billion and true free cash flow of $9.21 billion.

Using these estimates, as well as the results for the company for the 2025 fiscal year, we can see in the chart above how shares are priced right now. These aren't bad multiples, especially for such a high-quality growth machine that generates significant cash flows. But as the first chart below illustrates, the company is near the more expensive end of the spectrum. When stacked against five other similar firms, four of them ended up being cheaper on a price-to-operating-cash-flow basis. And three of them were cheaper when it came to the EV-to-EBITDA approach. On its own, this would not be enough for me to take a more neutral approach for the business. But in the second chart below, you can see that Enbridge has a net leverage ratio that is higher than any of the five institutions I compared it to. That means that, all else being the same, its risk profile is worse.

Of course, my decision on the company could change as new data becomes available. However, I am not convinced that is terribly likely. In the table below, you can see that analysts actually anticipate a decline in revenue from $13.35 billion last year to $13.32 billion this year. Meanwhile, earnings per share should fall from $0.74 to $0.71. That would mean a decline in net profits from $1.63 billion to $1.55 billion. In all likelihood, other cash flow metrics will worsen also. So unless management comes out with some major positive development, I think that a more cautious approach is likely justified.

Takeaway

Based on all the data that's available right now, it is my belief that Enbridge remains an interesting prospect. But I do think the easy money has been made now. Compared to other opportunities that exist in the market, it's just a decent prospect. Because of this, I believe that downgrading it to a "Hold" is wholly appropriate.

data.title: Bullish Flag Formation In Copper As Second U.S.-Iran Peace Talks Loom
data.description:
By Kelvin Wong

Copper (XCU/USD) has also benefited from a revival of risk-on appetite in the past seven trading sessions due to the temporary two-week ceasefire agreement between the US and Iran since 8 April 2026 to negate the risk of stagflation driven by fears of a prolonged global oil supply disruption.

Dr Copper has benefited from recent risk-on behaviour

Copper has rallied by 17% from the 23 March 2026 low of 5.1889 to print a two-month high of 6.1037 on 15 April 2026 as market participants look forward to a peace deal resolution between the US and Iran, erasing its early loss of 14% from the onset of the US-Iran war. Based on the 27 February 2026 pre-war baseline till Monday, 20 Apr 2026, LME spot copper has traded almost unchanged at -0.75% (see Fig. 1).

In the past three days, a game of poker has emerged as both sides are trying to build a “stronger hand” ahead of the second round of peace talks to take place in Pakistan, either on Tuesday or Wednesday, as stated by US President Trump via a media interview.

The US has continued to enact a naval blockade on Iranian-registered oil tankers in the Strait of Hormuz, and Iran has continued to force a closure in the strait to prevent international vessels from transporting and obtaining oil supplies in the GCC region.

Global manufacturing PMI is the second driver to watch

The longer-term price movement of copper is highly sensitive to manufacturing activities, as it is a vital component in the global manufacturing supply chains and industrial usage. Higher manufacturing activities tend to translate to a higher demand for copper, in turn, creating a positive feedback loop into the price of copper.

Manufacturing Purchasing Managers’ Index data compiled by S&P Global can be used as a leading indicator to gauge manufacturing activities.

Despite the rising risk of stagflation in the past six weeks since the start of the US-Iran war on 28 February 2026, on the aggregate, global manufacturing activities were still growing at a modest pace in March 2026.

The PMI Diffusion Index measures the proportion of countries whose manufacturing PMI is above or equal to 50 (an indication of expansion in activities), where it stood at 72.3% in March 2026, just down slightly from 74.5% in April 2026 (see Fig. 2).

Hence, there are still more than 50% of global economies’ manufacturing sectors in expansion modes, in turn, providing long-term bullish support for copper prices.

On Thursday, 23 April 2026, S&P Global will release flash PMI data for a slew of developed economies (Australia, Japan, the Eurozone, the UK, and the US). Therefore, a continuation of the expansion pace seen in these economies’ manufacturing PMIs is likely to maintain the ongoing short-term bullish trend of copper (XCU/USD).

Let's now focus on the short-term trajectory (1 to 3 days) of copper (XCU/USD) and its supporting elements from a technical analysis perspective.

Copper (XCU/USD) – Potential upside trigger at 6.0680

Copper (XCU/USD) has shaped a minor corrective pull-back of 2.65% from its recent two-month high of 6.1037 printed on 15 April 2026.

Watch the 5.8790 key short-term pivotal support, and a clearance of 6.0680 (upper boundary of the bullish flag) triggers the start of another potential bullish impulsive up-move sequence (see Fig. 3).

The next intermediate resistances stand at 6.1755 and 6.2465/6.2910 (also a Fibonacci extension).

On the other hand, a break and an hourly close below 5.8790 invalidates the bullish tone for another round of minor corrective decline to expose the next intermediate support zone of 5.7357/5.6545 (also the 20-day and 50-day moving averages).

Key elements to support the near-term bullish bias on copper (XCU/USD)

  • The recent corrective pull-back from the 15 April 2026 high of 6.1037 has taken the form of a bullish consolidation chart pattern called “bullish flag,” with its upper boundary acting as near-term resistance at 6.0680.
  • The price action of copper (XCU/USD) has continued oscillating within a minor ascending channel in place since the 23 March 2026 low and still has room to move towards the upper boundary of the channel at around 6.1755/6.2910.
  • The hourly RSI momentum indicator has managed to stage a rebound close to its support at the 32 level.

data.title: Amyloid Setback; Future Possibilities For The Treatment Of Alzheimer's Disease
data.description:
• A recent Cochrane review finds anti-amyloid antibody drugs like Biogen and Eisai’s Leqembi and Eli Lilly’s Kisunla offer only marginal cognitive and functional benefits for Alzheimer’s patients.

  • Given high costs, risks, and minimal efficacy, public health agencies may continue denying funding for these drugs, potentially impacting future sales if findings are confirmed.

  • Emerging research highlights a strong link between autism and Alzheimer’s, with peroxynitrite-mediated nitration as a shared pathological mechanism.

  • Antioxidant/peroxynitrite scavenging therapies, such as sulforaphane and Korean Red Ginseng, show promise for both Alzheimer’s and autism, suggesting a new therapeutic direction.

A couple of studies came out this April that while not likely to have much impact on companies working on Alzheimer’s treatments, may in the long run have a major impact on how Alzheimer’s disease (and potentially other diseases) are treated.

The first was a Cochrane review of anti-amyloid drugs for Alzheimer’s disease. The author’s reached the following conclusions:

The research found that the absolute effects of anti-amyloid drugs on cognitive decline and dementia severity were absent or trivial, falling well below established threshold for the minimum clinically important difference.

Defenders of the amyloid hypothesis of Alzheimer’s disease argued that this review did a disservice to FDA approved anti-amyloid antibody drugs - - namely Biogen (BIIB) and Eisai’s (ESALF) Leqembi and Eli Lilly’s (LLY) Kisunla - - as they were lumped in with failed anti-amyloid treatments from the past (critiques). However, Leqembi and Kisunla are only marginally better than these previous anti-amyloid antibodies. APOE4 single carriers who in most cases respond best to Leqembi and Kisunla saw only a slight slowing down of cognitive and functional decline versus placebo (as measured by the Clinical Dementia Ratings - Sum of Boxes score). For example, with Leqembi APOE4 single carriers with mild cognitive impairment to very mild Alzheimer’s disease declined by around .6 points less than those on placebo at eighteen months (for Kisunla the number was probably closer to one). A clinically significant change in CDR-SB scores is often put at between 1 and 2 points for this stage of the disease over this length of time (discussion). Given the expense, “inconvenience”, and potentially severe side effects from anti-amyloid drugs such as brain swelling and brain bleeding, the risk is not worth the marginal benefit even for APOE4 single carriers.

The Cochrane review findings likely means that public health agencies that deny funding for Leqembi and Kisunla will continue to do so. It may also lead some more doctors, caregivers, and patients to reject these treatments. This is unlikely, however, to have much effect on Biogen, Eisai, and Eli Lilly’s bottom line or their stock prices. If this Cochrane review is confirmed, though, by future third party data releases, it may significantly weaken sales for these drugs and may eventually even undermine the whole anti-amyloid approach to Alzheimer’s disease.

The other recent study focused on the growing evidence for the similarities between Alzheimer’s disease and autism. This study further indicated that individuals with autism are up to eight times more likely to develop Alzheimer’s disease than those without autism (research).

Anavex (AVXL) honed in on the conclusion that autophagy (the removal of misfolded amyloid and tau proteins, cellular debris, and synaptic pruning) is disrupted in both autism and Alzheimer's disease. Its drug blarcamesine is supposed to partially restore autophagy.

Decreased autophagy, though, in autism and Alzheimer’s disease is likely the result of a larger process: peroxynitrite-mediated nitration. Anavex may potentially and partially address this issue, but not as well as other future treatments. In any case, it looks like Anavex has no other choice now but to run a large phase 3 clinical trial for blarcamesine which will take several years.

The findings of a link between autism and Alzheimer’s disease are almost as important, if not more important, than those regarding the ineffectiveness of anti-amyloid drugs.

The following diagram shows the important pathways involved in autism and Alzheimer’s disease (ONOO- is peroxynitrite; PI or PIP2 is phosphatidylinositol 4,5 bisphosphate):

This pathway leads to cell death without cell growth due to the nitration of the phoshatidyinositol-3 kinase and the subsequent inactivation of Akt. On the other hand, the nitration and/or nitrosylation of the Phosphatase and TENsin homolog or PTEN leads to cell growth without cell death via overactivation of Akt which can result in cancer (although this may potentially occur before the nitration of the phosphatidylinositol-3 kinase in Alzheimer's disease and autism as well). Thus, individuals with Alzheimer’s disease and autism are much less likely to develop cancer.

Peroxynitrite scavengers via the production of water de-nitrates PTEN and the PI3 kinase, and other critical enzymes. Here’s an example of the potential effects of one peroxynitrite scavenger on autism and another one on Alzheimer’s disease.

All five clinical trials included in this systematic review showed a significant positive correlation between SFN [sulforphane] use and ASD [Autism Spectrum Disorder] behavior and cognitive function. The current evidence shows with minimal side effects observed, SFN appears to be a safe and effective treatment option for treating ASD (review).

At 24wk, there had been a significant improvement in KRG [Korean Red Ginseng/Panax ginseng]-treated groups. In the long-term evaluation of the efficacy of KRG after 24 wk, the improved MMSE (Mini-Mental State Examination] score remained without significant decline at the 48th and 96th wk. ADAS-cog [Alzheimer’s Disease Assessment Scale-cognition] showed similar findings. Maximum improvement was found around week 24. In conclusion, the effect of KRG on cognitive functions was sustained for 2 yr follow up, indicating feasible efficacies of long-term follow-up for Alzheimer’s disease (clinical trial).

De-nitration in neurodegenerative and neurodevelopmental diseases reduces cellular death, increases blood flow in the brain, and improves internal antioxidant systems. Peroxynitrite scavengers via anti-oxidation also modify immune responses and increase levels of neurotransmitters need for cognition, sleep, social recognition, alertness, and balanced mood.

Two studies: one showing the ineffectiveness of anti-amyloid drugs; the other showing the links between autism and Alzheimer’s disease point the way forward for the treatment of these diseases and perhaps others as well. There is very little out there now in terms of drug treatments for Alzheimer’s disease and autism, but the best antioxidants/de-nitrating agents (which may vary depending on disease type) provide hope for those suffering from several diseases that are currently either untreatable or poorly treated.
